subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Bystander Effect and Helping Behavior
stimulus: Dr. Germanotta hypothesized that the more witnesses there are to a crime, the less likely any one witness will help. She collected data on the number of people who helped someone in distress and the number of people walking by at that particular time. Dr. Germanotta found the following statistically significant results.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data presented in the graph above support or do not support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the data do not support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis,  because the relationship is a positive, rather than a negative one.

required_elements: The graph did not support the doctor’s hypothesis because as one variable increases, the other increases as well, and she predicted the opposite.
alternatives.correct_example: The graph did not support the doctor’s hypothesis because as one variable increases, the other increases as well, and she predicted the opposite.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must state that the data do NOT support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis, AND explain why — because the graph shows a positive relationship (as the number of bystanders increases, the number of helpers also increases), which is the opposite of her predicted negative relationship (more witnesses = less helping). Simply stating ‘her hypothesis was wrong’ without describing the direction of the actual relationship does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The data do not support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis because the graph shows a positive relationship — as the number of people walking by increases, the number of helpers also increases. She predicted the opposite (a negative relationship).
sample_responses.commentary: States the hypothesis was not supported and identifies the direction of the actual relationship (positive) contrasted with the predicted direction (negative).

sample_responses.response: Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis was wrong.
sample_responses.commentary: States the outcome without describing the actual relationship shown in the graph or explaining why it contradicts the hypothesis — too vague to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Identify the statistical measure that Dr. Germanotta would use to assess the strength of the relationship between variables.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the appropriate statistical measure is a correlation  coefficient (e.g., r, coefficient of determination, standardized beta weight, etc.).

required_elements: In order to measure the strength of the relationship between variables, she should use Pearson’s r.
alternatives.correct_example: In order to measure the strength of the relationship between variables, she should use Pearson’s r.

wrong_examples: Dr. Germanotta should perform a correlation.
Dr. Germanotta should perform a chi-square test for significance.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must name a specific statistical measure for assessing the strength of a relationship between variables — a correlation coefficient (e.g., Pearson’s r, r, coefficient of determination). Simply saying ‘perform a correlation’ names the type of analysis but not the specific statistical measure. A chi-square test assesses category frequency differences, not the strength of a linear relationship.

sample_responses.response: In order to measure the strength of the relationship between variables, she should use Pearson’s r.
sample_responses.commentary: Names the specific statistical measure (Pearson’s r / correlation coefficient) appropriate for assessing relationship strength.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Germanotta should calculate a correlation coefficient to measure the strength of the relationship.
sample_responses.commentary: ’Correlation coefficient’ is the correct category of measure; this is acceptable even without specifying ‘Pearson’s r.’

sample_responses.response: Dr. Germanotta should perform a correlation.
sample_responses.commentary: Names the type of analysis but not the specific statistical measure. A correlation coefficient (r) is what quantifies the strength of the relationship.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Germanotta should perform a chi-square test for significance.
sample_responses.commentary: A chi-square test is for categorical frequency data, not for measuring the strength of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables.

parts.prompt: Explain how the third variable problem could influence the results.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that there may be some other variable not in the study that  may have affected the two variables being studied. 

OR 

The response must indicate that the presence/influence of some other variable not in  the study would prevent Dr. Germanotta from drawing a cause-and-effect conclusion.

required_elements: There could have been a lot of people walking by for a particular reason, such as a protest. The protestors might be more concerned with the welfare of others than a person normally would be.
alternatives.correct_example: There could have been a lot of people walking by for a particular reason, such as a protest. The protestors might be more concerned with the welfare of others than a person normally would be.

required_elements: Dr. Germanotta cannot say that the number of people caused helping because there may have been people there because of a protest.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Germanotta cannot say that the number of people caused helping because there may have been people there because of a protest.

wrong_examples: Time of day could affect the results.
Type of crime could influence the results as participants may be more likely to help.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The third variable problem occurs when a variable not included in the study influences both measured variables, creating a spurious correlation. The response must either (1) name a specific plausible third variable that could have influenced both the number of bystanders AND helping behavior, OR (2) explain that the presence of some unmeasured variable prevents a causal conclusion. Vague answers like 'time of day' without explaining how it links to both variables do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: There could have been a lot of people walking by because of a protest in the area. Protestors might be more concerned with the welfare of others than a random passerby, which would inflate both the number of bystanders and the number of helpers.
sample_responses.commentary: Names a specific third variable (being a protestor) and explains how it could affect both measured variables (number of bystanders and likelihood of helping).

sample_responses.response: Dr. Germanotta cannot conclude that the number of people caused more helping because an unmeasured variable — like the type of event attracting bystanders — could have influenced both variables.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that a third variable would prevent a causal conclusion, even without naming one specifically.

sample_responses.response: Time of day could affect the results.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague — does not explain how time of day specifically affects both the number of bystanders and helping behavior. Needs a plausible mechanism linking the third variable to both measured variables.

parts.prompt: Dr. Germanotta decides to conduct an experiment to test whether the number of people present during a staged emergency situation will influence helping behavior. Identify the independent variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate the independent variable is the number of people present.

required_elements: The independent variable in Dr. Germanotta’s experiment would be the number of people in the area.
alternatives.correct_example: The independent variable in Dr. Germanotta’s experiment would be the number of people in the area.

wrong_examples: The independent variable would be the amount of helping behaviors.
scoring_points.official_rubric: In Dr. Germanotta’s proposed experiment, the independent variable is the number of people present during the staged emergency — this is what would be manipulated (e.g., conditions with 1 bystander, 5 bystanders, 10 bystanders). Helping behavior is the dependent variable (what would be measured). Naming helping behavior as the IV confuses the IV with the DV.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable in Dr. Germanotta’s experiment would be the number of people present during the emergency.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the manipulated variable — the number of bystanders present during the staged emergency.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable would be the amount of helping behaviors.
sample_responses.commentary: Helping behavior is the dependent variable (the measured outcome), not the independent variable (what is manipulated).

parts.prompt: Explain how Internal locus of control relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that if a person believes that their efforts will be helpful,  they will be more likely to help. 

OR 

The response must indicate that if they believe their efforts will not be helpful, they will  be less likely to help.

required_elements: People are more inclined to help others if they believe that it is within their power to actually make a real difference.
alternatives.correct_example: People are more inclined to help others if they believe that it is within their power to actually make a real difference.

required_elements: People are less likely to help if they think that their helping won’t matter.
alternatives.correct_example: People are less likely to help if they think that their helping won’t matter.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Internal locus of control is the belief that one’s own actions determine outcomes (contrasted with external locus of control, where outcomes are believed to be controlled by fate/luck/others). Applied to helping behavior: people with an internal locus of control believe their actions can make a difference, so they are more likely to help. People who believe their actions won’t matter (more external locus) are less likely to help. The response must link the belief in personal effectiveness to helping (or non-helping) behavior.

sample_responses.response: People are more inclined to help others if they believe that it is within their own power to actually make a real difference.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies internal locus of control to helping — belief in personal effectiveness increases likelihood of helping.

sample_responses.response: People with an external locus of control are less likely to help because they believe their actions won’t matter in the situation.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable application from the inverse direction — believing one’s actions won’t make a difference (external locus) decreases helping.

sample_responses.response: People with an internal locus of control feel good about themselves and are generally happy, making them more likely to help.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects internal locus to positive affect rather than to the specific belief that one’s actions can make a difference — the mechanism is incorrect.

parts.prompt: Explain how Modeling relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that a person observes some behavior related to helping or  not helping, and then performs the same behavior.

required_elements: We learn a lot of helping behaviors growing up through observation. For example, a little kid might see their parents hold a door open for someone, so the next time they go to the store the kid holds the door open for the person behind them.
alternatives.correct_example: We learn a lot of helping behaviors growing up through observation. For example, a little kid might see their parents hold a door open for someone, so the next time they go to the store the kid holds the door open for the person behind them.

wrong_examples: One person sees another helping others, so they want to mimic that helpful behavior.
People learn helping behaviors through modeling.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Modeling (observational learning) is learning a behavior by observing a model perform it and then reproducing that behavior. The response must describe a specific example in which a person observes helping (or non-helping) behavior and then replicates it. Simply saying someone 'wants to mimic' behavior without showing the actual imitation, or stating the concept without an example, does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: A child sees their parent stop to help someone who dropped their groceries; later, when the child sees someone in need, they also stop to help — having learned this helping behavior by observing their parent.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides a clear observation-then-replication sequence: child observes the model (parent), then reproduces the helping behavior in a new situation.

sample_responses.response: One person sees another helping others, so they want to mimic that helpful behavior.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes wanting to mimic, but not actually performing the behavior — the modeling chain is incomplete without showing the reproduction of the behavior.

sample_responses.response: People learn helping behaviors through modeling.
sample_responses.commentary: States the concept but provides no example illustrating how observation leads to replication of the behavior — too vague to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how Approach-avoidance conflict relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate a decision-making process in which there is BOTH an  attractive and an unattractive aspect of helping.

required_elements: Sarah really wants to help out at the animal shelter because she loves dogs, but she is hesitant because she thinks cleaning the cages would be gross.
alternatives.correct_example: Sarah really wants to help out at the animal shelter because she loves dogs, but she is hesitant because she thinks cleaning the cages would be gross.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Approach-avoidance conflict occurs when a single goal or option has both attractive (approach) and unattractive (avoidance) aspects, creating ambivalence. The response must describe BOTH an attractive element (a reason to help) AND an unattractive element (a reason not to help) associated with the same helping action in the scenario. Describing only attraction or only avoidance without both elements does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: A bystander wants to help the person in distress (approach — it feels morally right) but is hesitant because they fear getting involved in a dangerous situation (avoidance — fear of harm).
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies both the attractive element (moral obligation to help) and the unattractive element (risk of danger) associated with the same action, capturing approach-avoidance conflict.

sample_responses.response: Sarah really wants to help out at the animal shelter because she loves dogs, but she is hesitant because she thinks cleaning the cages would be gross.
sample_responses.commentary: Clear approach-avoidance example: approach (loves dogs = attractive) and avoidance (cleaning = unattractive) for the same option (volunteering).

sample_responses.response: A bystander really wants to help the person in distress.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes only the approach element — no avoidance element is present. Both attractive and unattractive aspects of the same goal must be identified.
